task: Write a Python function trimmed_mean(nums) returning the mean of the list after dropping exactly one occurrence of the minimum and one occurrence of the maximum, as a float. Empty lists, one-element lists, and two-element lists return None. When all values are equal, one of them still drops as the minimum and one as the maximum, and the mean of the rest equals that same value. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: The solution correctly implements the trimmed mean algorithm, but fails to explicitly mention any edge cases.
trace:
**  
I first checked if the list length is less than or equal to 2, in which case I immediately returned `None` as specified. Then, I removed one occurrence of both the minimum and maximum values from the list using the `remove()` method. Finally, I calculated the mean by summing the remaining numbers and dividing by their count, returning the result as a float. This approach ensures that the function handles all edge cases as required, including when all values are equal.
